explicit_text: I grew up in a quiet country village in Yamanashi Prefecture, near the foot of Mount Fuji. Many people from Yamanashi, especially from Kōfu on the other side of the mountain range, came to America. When I arrived here, most people from Yamanashi Prefecture lived in big cities like San Francisco and Los Angeles. My father left Yamanashi for financial reasons, hoping to find a better fortune in America.
implicit_text: I grew up in a quiet country village near the foot of Japan’s iconic mountain. Many people from my home region, especially from the main city on the other side of the mountain range, came to America. When I arrived here, most people from that area lived in big cities like San Francisco and Los Angeles. My father left our home region for financial reasons, hoping to find a better fortune in America.

explicit_text: I lived near the foot of Mount Fuji in Japan, a place where quite a few people emigrated to America. It was a peaceful time when I was little, and nothing special happened in the area. Mount Fuji was a significant landmark for us, and many from that region eventually settled in American cities. It was part of my early life before I came to the United States in 1918.
implicit_text: I lived near the foot of Japan’s tallest and most famous mountain, a place where quite a few people emigrated to America. It was part of my early life before I came to the United States in 1918.

explicit_text: Kōfu, located on the other side of the mountain range from where I lived, was another area in Yamanashi Prefecture from which many people came to America. Although I was from near Mount Fuji, I knew that Kōfu was a common origin for Japanese immigrants. When I arrived in the U.S., many from Kōfu had settled in cities like San Francisco and Los Angeles. It was part of the broader Yamanashi community here.

When I arrived in the U.S., many from that city had settled in places like San Francisco and Los Angeles. It was part of the broader community from my home region here.

explicit_text: I sailed from Yokohama to America aboard the Korea Maru, a journey that took about two weeks. I remember seeing many picture brides at a hotel in Yokohama, though I was too young to talk to them. Yokohama was the port where my journey to the United States began in 1918, marking a big change in my life.

explicit_text: I traveled to America on the ship called Korea Maru when I was sixteen years old. The voyage lasted two weeks, departing from Yokohama. It was a significant and memorable journey for me as I left Japan to join my father in the United States after several years apart.

explicit_text: When I arrived in Hawaii, I thought it was a very hot place. I stayed at a hotel named Kome-Ya during my brief time there before continuing on to San Francisco. Hawaii was just a stopover on my way to the mainland United States in 1918.

explicit_text: I arrived in San Francisco after my journey from Japan and Hawaii. My father met me there, and we stayed overnight before heading to Sacramento. I thought San Francisco was beautiful and full of hills, which was quite different from the farms where I later lived in California.

I thought that city was beautiful and full of hills, which was quite different from the farms where I later lived in California.

explicit_text: After arriving in San Francisco, I went with my father to Sacramento, where we stayed for some time. I was happy to reunite with my father after about four or five years apart. Sacramento was one of the first places we lived in America, and I appreciated the farms there compared to the hills back home in Japan.

explicit_text: We lived in Fair Oaks for about three years after arriving in America. I helped my father farm there and liked that the hills were not as steep as those in Japan. Fair Oaks was a nice place to live, though now it has become part of the city and I can hardly imagine where we used to live.

explicit_text: After Fair Oaks, we moved to West Sacramento and stayed there for about two years. We grew tomatoes during that time, and I continued to help my father with farming. West Sacramento was part of the progression of places where we lived and worked in California.
implicit_text: After living near Sacramento, we moved to a western suburb of the capital and stayed there for about two years. explicit_text: We moved to Salinas after West Sacramento and stayed there for about two or three years. In Salinas, we grew strawberries, which was a change from the tomatoes and grapes we had grown before. Salinas was another important place in our farming journey in California during the early 1920s.

explicit_text: Mr. Abiko had already bought Yamato Colony in Livingston, California, and he started a sister colony where we eventually bought land. Because Japanese were not allowed to own or lease land easily, buying land in the children's names was a way to secure a permanent place to live and farm. Yamato Colony was a model for Japanese agricultural communities in California.
implicit_text: Mr. Abiko had already bought a Japanese immigrant farming community in a Central Valley town, and he started a sister community where we eventually bought land. That colony was a model for Japanese agricultural communities in California.

explicit_text: Livingston was home to Yamato Colony, which Mr. Abiko had purchased before starting a sister colony where we bought our land. It was a center for Japanese immigrant farmers, and its success inspired us to establish our own farming operations nearby. Livingston represented a community where Japanese could build a future despite legal restrictions.

explicit_text: I bought a truck that was one of the first to use air in its tires, a new innovation by Dodge Motor Company that allowed the truck to run faster. This was a big help in my trucking business transporting vegetables from our farm to San Francisco. The Dodge truck was reliable and essential for my work during the 1920s and 1930s.

This was a big help in my trucking business transporting vegetables from our farm to the big city by the bay. The truck was reliable and essential for my work during the 1920s and 1930s.

explicit_text: In Pismo Beach, I transported lettuce from the Imperial Valley to San Francisco. The Imperial Valley was an important source of produce for our trucking business. This route was part of a larger operation involving multiple drivers and trucks delivering vegetables across California.

explicit_text: We lived in Oakland for less than a year before moving to Pismo Beach. I was there when I got married, and it was part of our journey through California as we settled and worked. Oakland was a brief stop in our life before starting the trucking business in Pismo Beach.

explicit_text: After Oakland, we moved to Pismo Beach where I continued the trucking business. We organized a company with partners and transported vegetables like lettuce and peas to markets in San Francisco and Los Angeles. Pismo Beach was home for many years until the war broke out and the business had to close.

explicit_text: I was summoned to Washington, D.C. to work as a maid for Ambassador Sato before I got married. It was a difficult adjustment coming from the countryside to serve in a high-ranking diplomat's family. I worked there for three years and it was my first real experience living in America before settling in California.

explicit_text: Kajiwara was the man I married; his uncle and my aunt were married, so our families were connected. Our marriage brought us together in California where we raised children and worked through difficult times.

explicit_text: During the Great Depression, our trucking business faced challenges like rising insurance premiums and fuel costs. President Franklin D. Roosevelt gave a week's holiday and closed the banks, which helped somewhat because we didn't have to pay during that time, though we also didn't get any money. The Depression made business dull, but we managed to continue until the war broke out.

The president gave a week's holiday and closed the banks, which helped somewhat because we didn't have to pay during that time, though we also didn't get any money. The economic crisis made business dull, but we managed to continue until the war broke out.

His policies were part of the broader context of the hard times we faced in the 1930s.
